project_overview.project_name: Nigeria Improved Child Survival Program for Human Capital MPA (IMPACT) - Phase 1
project_overview.project_development_objective: Improve the utilization and quality of immunization plus and malaria services in selected states
project_overview.total_financing: US$650.0 million equivalent (SDR 470.1 million IDA Credit)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Under-five children and women in participating states; health workers; government health system staff; 18 participating states representing 55% of under-five deaths in Nigeria

primary_locations: Malaria Control States: Abia, Borno, Ekiti, Imo, Lagos, Rivers (6 WB-financed); Adamawa, Bauchi, Kaduna, Kano, Sokoto (5 parallel-financed)
Immunization Plus States: Adamawa, Benue, Ebonyi, Kogi, Kwara, Nasarawa, Oyo, Plateau, Bauchi, Kaduna, Kano, Sokoto (12 states)
geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with focus on 18 selected states
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - North East (Borno) affected by Boko Haram insurgency; Niger Delta (Rivers) affected by security threats; project includes innovative approaches for security-compromised areas including mobile clinics and unannounced campaigns

key_components.description: Strengthening service delivery through performance-based contracts with NGOs for LLIN distribution, early treatment, IPT for pregnant women, SMC for children, and behavior change communication. Health systems strengthening including training, technical support, and performance frameworks for state malaria programs. Procurement of malaria commodities (LLINs, ACTs, RDTs, SP, SPAQ-SMC).

Strengthening service delivery through decentralized facility financing (DFF) to PHC facilities for immunization, maternal, child and neonatal services. Health systems strengthening including TA for DFF implementation, quality improvement, supervision systems, and performance frameworks. Vaccine procurement (PCV, rotavirus, meningococcal), cold chain strengthening, and polio operations support through UNICEF and WHO.

Strengthening M&E systems through LQAS surveys, household surveys (SMART), health facility surveys (NHFS), data quality assessments, GIS-based microplanning, and climate and health vulnerability assessments. Social behavior change communication campaigns through mass media and religious/traditional leaders. Learning agenda including operations research, randomized controlled trials, and impact evaluations of innovations.

Contingent financing mechanism to respond quickly to health emergencies with potential for major adverse economic/social impacts. Unused IDA financing allocated to this component in emergencies. Triggers and disbursement procedures outlined in Project Implementation Manual.
key_components.financing: US$0 million (allocated from unused funds if needed)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Ministry of Health (FMoH) with National Primary Health Care Development Agency (NPHCDA) and National Malaria Elimination Program (NMEP) as primary implementing entities at federal level; State Ministries of Health, State Primary Health Care Development Agencies (SPHCDAs), and State Malaria Elimination Programs (SMEPs) at state level
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: National Steering Committee (NSC) chaired by Minister of Health; State Steering Committees (SSCs) chaired by State Commissioners of Health; National Project Management Team (NPMT) co-led by NMEP and NPHCDA staff; quarterly development partner coordination meetings through Inter-agency Coordinating Committee; annual results conferences; progress reporting to Governors' Forum
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised August 2018); STEP system for procurement management; 15% domestic preference margin for goods; sole sourcing of UNICEF and WHO for vaccine procurement and polio operations based on 16 years successful experience; performance-based contracts with NGOs; advance contracting and retroactive financing allowed up to SDR 86.39 million
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Annual SMART household surveys for utilization indicators; annual National Health Facility Surveys (NHFS) for quality indicators; semi-annual LQAS surveys for LGA-level performance; DHS/MICS every 2-3 years for mortality and coverage estimates; routine DHIS-2 data; quarterly performance evaluations; independent external audits annually; semi-annual interim financial reports; data quality assessments; GIS-based population mapping; climate and health vulnerability assessments

identified_risks.risk_description: Federal and state elections resulted in delays in decision-making during government transitions. Continuing insecurity in North East (Boko Haram) and Niger Delta (Niger Delta Avengers) pose political risks to implementation. Potential for flare-ups of suicide bombing and pipeline attacks.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Use of innovative service delivery approaches (mobile clinics, unannounced campaigns in markets and LGA border towns) proven effective in security-compromised areas; establishment of National and State Steering Committees for coordination; regular policy dialogue; flexibility in project design to adapt to context; demonstrated success in North East where Penta 3 coverage increased 22.9 percentage points despite insurgency

identified_risks.risk_description: Nigeria's economic recovery is weak following 2016 recession. Growth driven by oil sector remains vulnerable to price shocks. Non-oil industry and services continue to shrink. Federal and state governments face challenges with domestic resource mobilization, limiting ability to invest in social sectors. Oil dependency creates fiscal vulnerability.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: BHCPF implemented as statutory transfer from 2020 onwards, ensuring guaranteed earmarked funding for PHC with ability to roll over unspent funds; covenant in Financing Agreement to ensure domestic financing for vaccines gradually increases per Gavi Accountability Framework; World Bank support for State Fiscal Transparency and Accountability PforR (P162009) to increase state revenues; front-loading of MPA investments to help Nigeria through economic recovery; performance-based advancement to subsequent phases contingent on increased domestic financing

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: NPHCDA and NMEP have limited experience working together on finding synergies and collaboration. Minimal familiarity with contracting NGOs. Weak coordination between federal and state entities. Lack of experience with performance-based approaches and results-based contracts.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establishment of National and State Steering Committees and Project Management Teams to strengthen coordination; provision of technical assistance at federal and state levels for contract management, supervision, and data analysis; regular peer learning and team strengthening events; annual results conferences; capacity assessments of states to inform TA; performance frameworks to foster accountability; training on DFF implementation; strengthening of existing institutional structures rather than creating new ones

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial fiduciary risk due to country context, challenges in previous projects, geographically dispersed activities, and introduction of innovations. Risk of misuse of funds, weak financial controls, and procurement challenges. Complexity of managing multiple state-level PIUs and NGO contracts.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Reliance on established fiduciary arrangements with UN partners (UNICEF, WHO) for vaccine procurement and polio operations; performance-based contracts with NGOs with third-party verification of results through independent M&E firms; electronic transfers directly to health facility bank accounts; decentralized financing with facility autonomy and community oversight through ward development committees; annual external audits by independent auditors; semi-annual interim financial reports; monthly financial reports; FM and procurement assessments of NMEP and NPHCDA; development of financial and procurement manuals; first FM on-site supervision within 6 months of effectiveness; dual currency accounting (naira and US dollars); designated accounts in Central Bank of Nigeria and commercial banks; performance frameworks for federal and state staff

identified_risks.risk_category: Technical Design of Project or Program
identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of implementing multiple innovations simultaneously (DFF, performance-based contracts with NGOs, SBCC, conditional transfers, performance frameworks). Potential for implementation delays in launching new approaches. Risk of inadequate quality of care despite increased coverage.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Design builds on lessons from successful previous operations (NSHIP, Malaria Booster Project, Polio Eradication Project); phased approach allows for learning and adaptation; robust M&E systems including LQAS, household surveys, and facility surveys; learning agenda with operations research and impact evaluations; performance-based quality improvement mechanisms (40% of DFF based on quality); supervisory checklists and data-driven supervision systems; TA for quality improvement including mentoring and continuous quality improvement techniques; private sector engagement for quality strengthening; randomized controlled trials to test innovations

identified_risks.risk_description: Healthcare waste management risks from vaccination, drug distribution, and LLIN disposal. Potential for gender-based violence and sexual exploitation/abuse given NGO service delivery and direct beneficiary contact. Disability inclusion concerns. Climate-related health risks including malaria transmission changes, flooding, and drought.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) and Healthcare Waste Management Plan (HCWMP) prepared and disclosed; alignment with National Healthcare Waste Management Plan and Guidelines; safe disposal methods for expired LLINs through collection and treatment; GBV risk assessment conducted; codes of conduct for health workers and NGOs prohibiting GBV; GRM with channels for GBV-related grievances; training on GBV prevention; patient rights awareness; referral protocols for survivors; disability inclusion through universal access considerations and data collection using Washington Group standards; climate and health vulnerability assessment; climate-resilient interventions including mobile clinics, NGO service delivery, rotavirus vaccine for diarrhea, seasonal SBCC campaigns; low carbon procurement policy; energy efficiency guidelines for facilities; tree planting for cooling

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential resistance from communities to new health interventions or behavior change messages. Limited awareness of project benefits. Possible conflicts between traditional practices and health recommendations.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive social behavior change communication strategy using mass media, social media, and religious/traditional leaders; community mobilizer training (CHIPS); focus groups and workshops with beneficiaries to understand perceptions; grievance redress mechanism (Servicom) for feedback and complaints; engagement of ward development committees in facility management; community health worker involvement; formative research to inform SBCC design; adaptation of messages to cultural context; peer learning events for LGA-level results discussion

triggered_safeguard_policies: Environmental Assessment OP/BP 4.01 (triggered due to healthcare waste from vaccination, drug distribution, and LLIN disposal)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Program-based feedback mechanism and GRM to receive, address, and resolve complaints related to service quality and exclusion. Strengthening of Government's Servicom GRM. NGOs mandated to establish functional GRMs with annual independent assessment. Multiple channels for complaints including confidential reporting for GBV-related grievances. Referral mechanisms for services. World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Inspection Panel available for project-affected communities.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project promotes and mainstreams gender concerns in service delivery and capacity building. Ensures women's needs during antenatal, intrapartum, and postnatal periods are considered. Supports interventions benefiting both girls and boys through affordable access to health services. Deeper analysis of gender norms and barriers to women's access to health services. Gender-disaggregated data collection on key PDO indicators. Capacity-building interventions encourage female participation in health workforce. SBCC interventions reflect cultural peculiarity while addressing gender inequality. Postpartum family planning services included. Skilled birth attendance and postnatal care focus addresses neonatal mortality.
vulnerable_groups: Pregnant women and women of reproductive age
Poor and lowest income quintiles (project focuses on diseases and interventions disproportionately affecting poor children)
Rural populations (intensified interventions in rural areas with lower access)
Internally displaced persons (mobile clinics in North East)
Persons with disabilities (universal access considerations, disability data collection)
Women and girls (gender-specific barriers to health service access)
Unimmunized children (37% of global unvaccinated children live in Nigeria)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project acknowledges security challenges in North East (Boko Haram insurgency) and Niger Delta (Niger Delta Avengers). Incorporates conflict-sensitive approaches including: (1) Use of innovative service delivery mechanisms (mobile clinics, unannounced campaigns in markets and LGA border towns using entertainment) proven effective in security-compromised areas; (2) Engagement of NGOs for service delivery in hard-to-reach areas; (3) Flexibility in project design to adapt to changing security context; (4) Evidence of success: Penta 3 coverage increased 22.9 percentage points in North East despite ongoing insurgency; (5) Project design allows for rapid response to Government requests and adaptation to context; (6) Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC) for emergency response to health crises; (7) Focus on reaching most isolated and vulnerable populations through diverse service delivery mechanisms
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Nigeria faces multiple fragility factors: (1) Political fragility: Recent federal and state elections caused implementation delays; weak governance and coordination between federal and state entities; (2) Security fragility: Boko Haram insurgency in North East with ongoing suicide bombings; Niger Delta Avengers pipeline attacks; (3) Economic fragility: Weak economic recovery post-2016 recession; oil-dependent economy vulnerable to price shocks; high unemployment (23%) and underemployment (20%); 100 million Nigerians living on less than US$1.90/day; (4) Social fragility: High inequality with 80% of poor in northern Nigeria; gender norms limiting women's mobility and health access; high fertility rate (5.3 children per woman) creating demographic pressures; (5) Health system fragility: Extremely high under-five mortality (132 per 1,000 births, highest for lower-middle-income countries); 26% of Sub-Saharan Africa's under-five deaths occur in Nigeria; low health spending (0.6% of GDP); weak institutional capacity; limited coordination between health agencies; (6) Environmental fragility: Vulnerability to flooding (southern regions, Niger Delta) and drought (northern regions); climate change affecting malaria transmission patterns; desertification in southern regions
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project acknowledges security risks and incorporates mitigation measures: (1) North East (Borno state) affected by Boko Haram insurgency - project uses mobile clinics and community-based campaigns to maintain service delivery; (2) Niger Delta (Rivers state) affected by Niger Delta Avengers - project includes security-conscious implementation approaches; (3) Project design allows flexibility to adapt to security context changes; (4) Use of NGOs for service delivery in remote/insecure areas; (5) Contingent Emergency Response Component for rapid response to crises; (6) Evidence of implementation success in insecure areas: immunization coverage increased despite security challenges; (7) Project includes support for internally displaced populations through mobile health services
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Project acknowledges internally displaced persons (IDPs) in North East due to Boko Haram insurgency. Specific mitigation: Component 1 (Malaria Control) Subcomponent 1.1 includes provision of mobile health services to internally displaced populations to enhance health care access at local level. Project design incorporates flexibility to serve displaced populations through innovative service delivery mechanisms including mobile clinics and community-based campaigns. However, detailed assessment of refugee/IDP populations and specific targeting mechanisms not extensively detailed in PAD.
